elements.sent-2411-001: Trust and reciprocation of it form the foundation of economic, social and other interactions.
elements.sent-2411-002: While the Trust Game is widely used to study these concepts for interactions between two players, often alternating different roles (i.e., investor and trustee), its extensions to multi-player scenarios have been restricted to instances where players assume only one role.
elements.sent-2411-003: We propose a symmetric N-player Trust Game, in which players alternate between two roles, and the payoff of the player is defined as the average across their two roles and drives the evolutionary game dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-004: We find that prosocial strategies are harder to evolve with the present symmetric N-player Trust Game than with the Public Goods Game, which is well studied.
elements.sent-2411-005: In particular, trust fails to evolve regardless of payoff function nonlinearity in well-mixed populations in the case of the symmetric N-player trust game.
elements.sent-2411-006: In structured populations, nonlinear payoffs can have strong impacts on the evolution of trust.
elements.sent-2411-007: The same nonlinearity can yield substantially different outcomes, depending on the nature of the underlying network.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our results highlight the importance of considering both payoff structures and network topologies in understanding the emergence and maintenance of prosocial behaviours.
elements.sent-2411-009: Researchers across disciplines have explored how prosocial behaviours evolve among self-interested individuals.
elements.sent-2411-010: A key focus is the evolution of cooperation in social dilemmas, such as the Prisoner's Dilemma (PD) game and its $N$-player variant, the Public Goods Game (PGG) \cite{Wei:2024aa, Ren:2024nr, Li:2017kx, Chiong:2012uq, nowak2006five, hauert2006synergy, Van-Segbroeck:2009uq, Sasaki:2012rz .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In particular, evolutionary game theory enables us to examine how successful strategies proliferate through evolution, i.e., fitness-dependent reproduction and imitation \cite{SMITH:1973aa, Taylor:1978aa .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Evolutionary game theory also has practical applications, including green supply chain policy formulation, modelling technology diffusion, and others \cite{Long:2021wt, Bolluyt:2019um, tang2015when .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Many real-world scenarios involve sequential interactions between two players, as seen in buyer-seller exchanges.
elements.sent-2411-014: Unlike the PD and PGG, which model simultaneous interactions, sequential interactions introduce a trust issue where one player's decision may leave them vulnerable to exploitation by the other \cite{Bravo:2008aa .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The concepts of trust and trustworthiness have also gained attention in engineering research \cite{Li:2023kq, Calegari:2024aa, Ting:2021rc, Braga:2018qf, Cho:2015aa .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Many problems in these fields are framed as buyer-seller interactions \cite{Jung:2019yq .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The Trust Game (TG) is a current standard for formalising non-simultaneous interactions under social dilemmas and is widely used for studying trust and trustworthiness \cite{Bravo:2008aa, Johnson:2011aa, Camerer:1988fk, Berg:1995aa, Masuda:2012aa, McNamara:2009aa, Tzieropoulos:2013aa, Kumar:2020aa, Capraro:tw .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The TG involves a one-shot sequential interaction between an investor (truster) and a trustee.
elements.sent-2411-019: The binary TG is a simple variant where each role has two strategies: investors can choose to invest or not, and trustees can choose to be trustworthy or untrustworthy \cite{Masuda:2012aa, Bohnet:2004xe, Guth:2000qq, dasgupta:2000aa .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Evolutionary game theory predicts that anti-social strategies evolve in the two-player TG, resulting in reduced payoffs for both players compared to prosocial strategies.
elements.sent-2411-021: Consequently, an additional mechanism is needed for prosocial strategies to evolve in the TG \cite{Manapat:2013aa, Tarnita:2015aa, Lim:2022vt .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In practice, interactions often involve multiple participants, so recent attention has been paid to $N$-player TG (NTG) \cite{Abbass:2016aa, Chica:2018wt, Hu:2021tl, Fang:2021ut, Lim:2024ty,Zhou:2024zr , which attempts to generalise the two-player TG to multiplayer settings, with $N\geq 3$.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Two alternative approaches exist for NTG, each with distinct numbers of strategies and imitation processes.
elements.sent-2411-024: The original NTG model employs three strategies: one for investors and two for trustees \cite{Abbass:2016aa .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In this model, investors can only invest, and trustees can choose to be trustworthy or untrustworthy.
elements.sent-2411-026: The evolutionary game dynamics are driven by role-independent imitation of strategies, wherein an investor can imitate a trustee's strategy and thus become a trustee, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-027: Subsequent NTG studies often adopt these assumptions \cite{Chica:2018wt,Hu:2021tl, Fang:2021ut, Sun:2022uq, Liu:2022fk, Xia:2022yq, Li:2023kx, Feng:2023fr,Liu:2024db .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Without additional mechanisms, the evolutionary outcome in a well-mixed population is the extinction of investors, with only trustees surviving.
elements.sent-2411-029: The lack of investment predicted by classical game theory aligns with the absence of surviving investors derived by this version of evolutionary game theory.
elements.sent-2411-030: An alternative approach is an asymmetric NTG model that employs four strategies, two per role \cite{Lim:2024ty .}
elements.sent-2411-031: In this model, investors can choose to invest or not.
elements.sent-2411-032: The evolutionary game dynamics are driven by role-dependent strategy imitation between investors or trustees, but not between an investor and a trustee.
elements.sent-2411-033: In this asymmetric NTG, players maintain fixed roles throughout the evolutionary process.
elements.sent-2411-034: Without additional mechanisms, the evolutionary outcome in a well-mixed population is the evolution of non-investing investors.
elements.sent-2411-035: The lack of investment predicted by classical game theory is consistent with the evolution of investors who do not invest in this version of evolutionary game theory.
elements.sent-2411-036: In both the original and asymmetric NTGs, single-role payoffs drive evolutionary game dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-037: As such, they may be less suitable for scenarios in which players alternate between the two roles (i.e., investor and trustee) and the average payoff over the two roles should drive the evolutionary dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-038: It is also worth noting that behavioural experiments often assign human participants to alternate between investor and trustee roles \cite{Burks:2003vn,Altmann:2008uq,Espin:2016fk .}
elements.sent-2411-039: We propose a symmetric NTG (SNTG) to model situations in which players alternate between the two roles.. Our contributions include:.
elements.sent-2411-040: Proposal of an SNTG in which players alternate between the two roles, with the average payoff from the two roles driving evolutionary game dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-041: Analysis of evolutionary dynamics of the SNTG in the infinite well-mixed population and two types of finite structured populations (i.e., square lattice and heterogeneous networks).
elements.sent-2411-042: Examination of interactions between payoff function nonlinearity and population structure, affecting evolution of trust.
elements.sent-2411-043: Evidence that high-degree nodes influence evolution of trust in heterogeneous networks, suggesting potentials for intervention.
elements.sent-2411-044: Principal differences between the original NTG and the proposed SNTG on networks are shown in Fig.\,\ref{fig_multiple_groups and Table \ref{tab_comparison .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Further details on these distinctions are provided in subsequent sections.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.49\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_multiple_groups} \end{center} \caption{Different definitions of the payoff in $N$-player games on networks. (a) The definition of the payoff for the present STNG. A focal player (the black circle) belongs to five groups (shown as the shaded area in each of the five copies of the local network). The focal player is assumed to earn payoffs from each group. The summed payoff drives evolutionary game dynamics, as is often the case for other $N$-player game dynamics on networks such as the PGG \cite{Santos:2008aa, Perc:2013aa}. (b) An alternative definition of the payoff in an $N$-player game on networks. With this definition, while the focal player belongs to the same five groups, one assumes that the focal player's payoff only originates from the one group centred around it. The network version of the original NTG uses this definition of the payoff \cite{Chica:2018wt}. } \label{fig_multiple_groups}

elements.sent-2411-046: Our SNTG expands upon the asymmetric NTG \cite{Lim:2024ty by symmetrising it.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Players alternate between investor and trustee roles, similar to symmetrising two-player asymmetric games \cite{Lim:2022vt,Hofbauer:2003aa,sigmund2016calculus .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Investors choose whether to invest or not, while trustees decide to be trustworthy or untrustworthy.
elements.sent-2411-049: A player's strategy therefore comprises two elements, one for each role: $i$ (i.e., invest) and $n$ (i.e., not to invest) as investor and $t$ (i.e., trustworthy) and $u$ (i.e., untrustworthy) as trustee.
elements.sent-2411-050: There are four possible strategies: $it, iu, nt$, and $nu$.
elements.sent-2411-051: A total of $N_I$ investors and $N_T$ trustees participate in an NTG, where $N = N_I + N_T$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Each player among the $N_I$ investors employs their investor strategy in the NTG; for example, an $it$ player within the $N_I$ investors acts as an investing investor.
elements.sent-2411-053: Similarly, each player among the $N_T$ trustees utilises their trustee strategy; for instance, an $it$ player within the $N_T$ trustees acts as a trustworthy trustee.
elements.sent-2411-054: In an NTG, the profit from the investment made by investing investors is equally distributed among trustees, and then the trustworthy trustees share this profit with the investing investors \cite{Lim:2024ty .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Given $N_I$ investors and $N_T$ trustees in an NTG, the payoffs $\Pi_i$, $\Pi_n$, $\Pi_t$, and $\Pi_u$ for investing investors, non-investing investors, trustworthy trustees and untrustworthy trustees, respectively, are \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \Pi_i(m_i,g_t) = \frac{g_t}{N_T} \frac{r\left(1-w^{m_i}\right)}{m_i(1-w)} +\frac{g_t}{N_T} -1, \quad \Pi_n = 0, \Pi_t(m_i) = r\frac{1}{N_T} \frac{1-w^{m_i}}{1-w}, \quad\quad\quad \Pi_u(m_i) = \frac{1}{r} \Pi_t(m_i), \end{aligned} \label{eq_payoffs_roles}\end{equation} where $m_i \in \{0,1,\ldots,N_I\}$ and $g_t \in \{0,1,\ldots,N_T\}$ are the numbers of investing investors and trustworthy trustees, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-056: Parameter $r$, satisfying $0 < r <1$, represents the productivity of prosocial strategies (i.e., investing investor and trustworthy trustee) relative to the payoff that an untrustworthy trustee receives from investing investors.
elements.sent-2411-057: Parameter $w$ $(>0)$ determines how the investment value accumulates.
elements.sent-2411-058: For $0<w<1$, each additional investor's contribution diminishes.
elements.sent-2411-059: At $w=1$, each investor's contribution is $1$ regardless of $m_i$ (obtained using L'H\^{o pital's rule).}
elements.sent-2411-060: For $w>1$, the per-investor contribution increases with $m_i$, representing synergistic benefits.
elements.sent-2411-062: We examine the SNTG in both well-mixed and structured populations, where the expected payoff of each player across the two different roles drives the evolutionary game dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-063: In a well-mixed, infinitely large population, we denote by $y_{it}$, $y_{iu}$, $y_{nt}$, and $y_{nu}$ the frequencies of $it$, $iu$, $nt$, and $nu$ types, respectively, with $y_{it +y_{iu +y_{nt +y_{nu =1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: From time to time, $N_I$ investors and $N_T$ trustees are randomly selected to participate in a one-shot NTG.
elements.sent-2411-065: We define $N \equiv N_I + N_T$ and fix the values of $N_I$ and $N_T$.
elements.sent-2411-066: For an investing investor of $it$ or $iu$ type, the investor part of the expected payoff $P_i$ is \begin{equation}P_i = -1 + (y_{it} + y_{nt}) \left[1 + \frac{r}{N_I(1-w)} \frac{1}{(y_{it} + y_{iu})} \left\{1 - \left[1 + (w-1)(y_{it} + y_{iu})\right]^{N_I}\right\}\right]. \label{eq_payoff_investing}\end{equation} Note that Eq.~\eqref{eq_payoff_investing requires that $y_{it +y_{iu}\ne0$ and $w\neq 1$.}}

elements.sent-2411-076: Occasionally, each player has the opportunity to imitate the strategy of another randomly chosen player.
elements.sent-2411-077: This imitation (also called social learning) process results in the following form of evolutionary game dynamics at the population level \cite{Sandholm:2010aa : \begin{equation}\dot{y}_{s} =\sum_{s^{\prime} \in \mathcal{S}-\{s\}}y_{s^{\prime}} y_{s}\hat{\rho}_{s^{\prime} \rightarrow s} -\sum_{s^{\prime} \in \mathcal{S}-\{s \}}y_{s}y_{s^\prime} \hat{\rho}_{s \rightarrow s^\prime}, \label{eq_dynamics}\end{equation} where the dot denotes the time derivative, $s, s^\prime \in \mathcal{S}\equiv\{it,iu,nt,nu\}$, and $\hat{\rho _{s^\prime \rightarrow s}$ is proportional to the probability of a transition from strategy $s^\prime$ to $s$ through imitation.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The first term represents the inflow of players to strategy $s$ from the other strategies.
elements.sent-2411-079: The second term represents the outflow of players from strategy $s$ to the other strategies.
elements.sent-2411-080: We employ the Fermi function for the strategy switching: \begin{equation}\hat{\rho}_{s_f\rightarrow s_m} \equiv\frac{1}{1+e^{-\beta (P_m-P_f)}}, \label{eq_Fermi}\end{equation} where $P_m$ and $P_f$ denote the expected payoffs of the mimicked player and mimicking player, respectively, and $\beta \ge 0$ denotes the selection strength.
elements.sent-2411-081: Equation~\eqref{eq_Fermi is widely used in evolutionary game dynamics \cite{Szabo:2007tg,Perc:2013aa .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Then, we obtain \begin{equation}\dot{y}_{s} =y_{s} \left[ \sum_{s^\prime \ne s} y_{s^{\prime}} \tanh \left(\frac{1}{2} \beta (P_{s} -P_{s^\prime})\right)\right], \label{eq_population_dynamics}\end{equation} where $s, s^\prime \in\mathcal{S $, and $\frac{1 {1+e^{-x -\frac{1 {1+e^x =\tanh(\frac{x {2 )$ is used.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Note that, given the simplex constraint $y_{it +y_{it +y_{nt +y_{nu =1$, we are left with three independent variables.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-085: In structured populations, individuals interact via edges of the given network.
elements.sent-2411-086: An SNTG is associated with a neighbourhood in the network, with each player usually belonging to multiple neighbourhoods.
elements.sent-2411-087: Given a player's node degree $d$, the player belongs to $d+1$ groups and thus participates in $d+1$ SNTGs: one associated with the player's own neighbourhood and those of the player's neighbours.
elements.sent-2411-088: A player's total payoff in one round is assumed to be the sum of the payoffs from all $d+1$ groups.
elements.sent-2411-089: Notably, the strategies of neighbours of the neighbours of player $j$ affect $j$'s total payoff.
elements.sent-2411-090: This fact distinguishes $N$-player games from 2-player games on networks; in the latter case, only $j$'s direct neighbours impact $j$'s total payoff.
elements.sent-2411-091: From a group of $N = d+1$ players defined by a node with degree $d$ and its neighbours in a network, we select $N_I$ players as investors uniformly at random.
elements.sent-2411-092: The remaining $N_T$ $(= N - N_I)$ players act as trustees.
elements.sent-2411-093: These investors and trustees then participate in an NTG to earn payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-094: Both $N_I$ and $N_T$ are fixed for each group.
elements.sent-2411-095: In a structured population, the group size $N$ can vary, depending on the node degree.
elements.sent-2411-096: Therefore, we determine $N_I$ per group using a single global parameter $p \in (0,1)$, which is an approximate probability that a player takes an investor role.
elements.sent-2411-097: Given $p$, we set $N_I =\min\left(\lceil Np \rceil, N-1\right)$, yielding $N_I \in\{1,\ldots,N-1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-098: This ensures that the NTG remains well-defined, as previously described for the well-mixed population.
elements.sent-2411-099: Due to the random selection of investors, the payoff for each strategy in this NTG is stochastic.
elements.sent-2411-100: Consequently, we focus on the expected payoffs, calculating one for each strategy in each group.
elements.sent-2411-101: Using these expected payoffs ensures that the players employing the identical strategy gain the same payoff from the NTG played within the group.

elements.sent-2411-112: We numerically run evolutionary dynamics on networks as follows.
elements.sent-2411-113: The simulation begins with an initial population of strategies, then repeatedly performs two steps: (1) We uniformly randomly select a player (i.e., node), denoted by $j$, and one of its neighbours, denoted by $k$.
elements.sent-2411-114: Unless we state otherwise, simulations commence with equal proportions of the four strategies ($it$, $iu$, $nt$, and $nu$) uniformly randomly distributed over the $Z = 1024$ nodes, which we refer to as the unbiased initial condition.
elements.sent-2411-116: In each generation, the two steps are repeated $Z$ times, allowing each node to update its strategy once per generation on average.
elements.sent-2411-117: For each parameter pair $(w,r)$, we ran 20 independent simulations.
elements.sent-2411-119: The first network is a square lattice of linear size $Z^{1/2 = 32$ with periodic boundary conditions and the von Neumann neighbourhood, which yields the degree of each node equal to $d=4$.}
elements.sent-2411-120: The second network is a realization of the Barab\'{a si-Albert (BA) model, which we refer to as a heterogeneous network; the network construction begins with a cycle graph containing 3 nodes, i.e., a triangle, and then we add a node with $m=2$ edges in each step of the preferential attachment algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-121: The average degree is equal to $\langle d\rangle \approx 3.994$, which is approximately the same as that for the square lattice by design.
elements.sent-2411-122: We generate a different heterogeneous network for each simulation.
elements.sent-2411-123: The BA model produces an approximately power-law degree distribution with power-law exponent $3$, which is in stark contrast with the square lattice in which all nodes have the same degree.
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.94\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_well_mixed_simplex} \end{center} \caption{ Evolutionary dynamics of the SNTG in the infinite well-mixed population, shown over the triangular faces of the 3-simplex $\triangle^3 = \{(y_{it},y_{iu},y_{nt},y_{nu}) : y_{it} +y_{iu} +y_{nt} +y_{nu}=1\}$ as the state space. Vertices IT, IU, NT, and NU correspond to homogeneous population states $y_{it}=1$, $y_{iu}=1$, $y_{nt}=1$, and $y_{nu}=1$, respectively. All trajectories converge to the line of stable equilibria on the NT-NU edge given by $y_{nt}+y_{nu}=1$ and $\frac{r}{r+1}< y_{nu}\le 1$. Therefore, investment (i.e. trust) does not evolve. elements.sent-2411-195: We propose the SNTG and analyse its evolutionary game dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-196: Each player is assumed to play both investor and trustee roles in each generation.
elements.sent-2411-197: Therefore, the mean payoff from both roles drives evolutionary dynamics, unlike the asymmetric NTG in which players have fixed roles (i.e., investor or trustee) and therefore different strategy sets over the evolutionary dynamics \cite{Lim:2024ty .}
elements.sent-2411-198: The SNTG reflects scenarios of role-switching.
elements.sent-2411-199: This approach aligns with behavioural experiments of TGs in which participants alternate roles \cite{Johnson:2011aa .}
elements.sent-2411-200: The original NTG is technically a symmetric game because all players share the same set of strategies \cite{Abbass:2016aa .}
elements.sent-2411-201: In that game, each player can switch the role as a result of payoff-driven imitation (e.g., an investor turns into an untrustworthy trustee).
elements.sent-2411-202: The original NTG, in which each player has a fixed role at any given time and single-role payoffs drive evolutionary game dynamics, fundamentally differs from the present SNTG, in which each player plays both roles in any generation and the sum of the payoffs from the two roles drives evolutionary dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-203: We have found that the SNTG is a challenging symmetric $N$-player game for prosocial behaviour to evolve.
elements.sent-2411-204: The PGG is also a challenging, and widely studied, symmetric $N$-player games for evolution of prosocial behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-205: However, it can still foster the evolution of prosocial behaviour in well-mixed populations using nonlinear payoff functions \cite{hauert2006synergy,archetti2012review,Hauert:2024qy .}
elements.sent-2411-206: In contrast, we have shown that the SNTG does not foster prosocial behaviour even with nonlinear payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-207: The asymmetric NTG has been shown to present greater challenges for the evolution of prosocial behaviours than the PGG \cite{Lim:2024ty .}
elements.sent-2411-208: In-depth comparisons of the SNTG and PGG may be a productive exercise because both are symmetric games.
elements.sent-2411-209: The interaction between nonlinearity and population structure differs between the SNTG and PGG.
elements.sent-2411-210: In both games, population structure alone promotes prosocial behaviours, even with linear payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-211: In the SNTG, nonlinearity catalyses the evolution of prosocial behaviours with network reciprocity but does not promote prosocial behaviours on its own.
elements.sent-2411-212: In the PGG, nonlinearity directly boosts prosocial behaviours \cite{li2016evolutionary .}
elements.sent-2411-213: Here, the interaction is between two boosting mechanisms.

elements.sent-2411-215: A majority of research comparing homogeneous and heterogeneous networks in social dilemma games focuses on linear payoff functions, typically using 2-player or linear $N$-player games \cite{Chica:2018wt,Santos:2008aa,Santos:2005ly,Santos:2006aa,Perc:2013aa .}

elements.sent-2411-219: We have also shown that, in heterogeneous networks, initialising based on the node degree can significantly enhance trust, offering the potential for effective interventions to promote prosocial behaviours.

elements.subsection-2411-020: Existing NTG Models and Rationale for Symmetry
elements.sent-2411-349: Existing NTG models, including the asymmetric NTG detailed previously, typically assume fixed roles with evolutionary dynamics driven by single-role payoffs.
elements.sent-2411-350: However, real-world interactions often involve fluid roles.
elements.sent-2411-351: Even in the standard 2-player TG context, which is inherently asymmetric, symmetrisation (where players alternate between investor and trustee roles) is well-established \cite{sigmund2016calculus, Lim:2022vt .}
elements.sent-2411-352: This approach reflects the common real-world occurrence of role alternation; for instance, individuals often act as both buyer (investor) and seller (trustee), suggesting that incorporating this dynamic is a worthwhile consideration for modelling trust dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-353: The relevance of role alternation arguably extends to complex, multi-player settings requiring collective action between groups.
elements.sent-2411-354: Consider, for instance, joint ventures or supply chain clusters where enterprises form a buying consortium (acting as multiple buyers/investors) to jointly buy materials or capacity, needing collective action for scale or access to specialised suppliers.
elements.sent-2411-355: This group of buyers decides whether to collectively engage with a selling alliance (acting as multiple sellers/trustees).
elements.sent-2411-356: Engaging involves placing orders (an act of investment) and implicitly trusting the seller group to act trustworthily, for example, by meeting quality and delivery standards, rather than acting untrustworthily.
elements.sent-2411-357: Crucially, participants often switch roles: the same enterprise in the buying consortium might simultaneously participate in a separate selling alliance (acting as one of multiple sellers/trustees) supplying components to another buying consortium.
elements.sent-2411-358: Such $N$-player scenarios, involving group interactions where entities operate as part of both investor groups and trustee groups, further highlight the potential limitations of fixed-role models.
elements.sent-2411-359: The frequent use of role alternation in behavioural TG experiments also points towards its perceived importance \cite{Burks:2003vn,Altmann:2008uq,Espin:2016fk .}
elements.sent-2411-360: Given these motivations for considering models with role alternation, a key question arises: how should evolutionary fitness be determined when individuals occupy multiple roles over time? If players frequently switch between being investors and trustees, their overall success likely depends on their performance across both activities.
elements.sent-2411-361: Indeed, existing models of symmetric 2-player TGs often address this by using the average payoff across roles to determine fitness \cite{sigmund2016calculus, Lim:2022vt .}
elements.sent-2411-362: Evaluating fitness based solely on performance in a single, temporarily held role may therefore be insufficient in dynamic settings.
elements.sent-2411-363: Extending this logic to the multi-player context, we propose a symmetric NTG (SNTG) where evolutionary fitness is explicitly determined by the average payoff accumulated across both the investor and trustee roles.
elements.sent-2411-364: This mechanism contrasts fundamentally with the single-role payoff calculations underpinning fixed-role models or the imitation dynamics in the original NTG.
elements.sent-2411-365: This paper formally defines this SNTG framework, motivated by the prevalence of role-switching and based on an average payoff structure analogous to that used in symmetric 2-player models, and investigates its evolutionary dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-366: We particularly explore how trust evolves within various population structures and interaction conditions under this symmetric approach, where prosocial outcomes depend on agents successfully navigating both the investor role (e.g., choosing to trust/invest) and the trustee role (e.g., choosing to be trustworthy).
elements.sent-2411-368: Players alternate between investor and trustee roles, similar to symmetrising 2-player asymmetric games \cite{Lim:2022vt,Hofbauer:2003aa,sigmund2016calculus .}

elements.figure-2411-013: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.94\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_initial_condition_138} \includegraphics[width=0.23\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_unit_32} \end{center} \caption{ Robustness of evolutionary outcomes to intial condition $\left(y_{it}(0), y_{iu}(0), y_{nt}(0), y_{nu}(0)\right)$, where $y_{iu}(0) = y_{nt}(0) =y_{nu}(0) =\frac{1}{3} \left(1-y_{it}(0)\right)$. Changes in the initial condition produce outcomes qualitatively similar to those observed under the baseline condition, $y_{it}(0)=y_{iu}(0)=y_{nt}(0)=y_{nu}(0)=1/4$. This robustness holds for the tested initial conditions which are biased against the most prosocial type, IT (i.e., beginning with a lower initial proportion of IT compared to the baseline). Note that outcomes remain qualitatively similar even when the initial proportion $y_{it}(0)$ of IT is reduced by over an order of magnitude (e.g., from 1/4 to 1/64). } \label{fig_initial_condition}
elements.figure-2411-014: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.94\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_selection_strength_137} \includegraphics[width=0.23\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_unit_32} \end{center} \caption{ Robustness of evolutionary outcomes to variations in selection strength, $\beta$. Changes in selection strength yield outcomes that are qualitatively similar to those observed under the baseline condition ($\beta=10$). Note that outcomes remain qualitatively similar even when the selection strength is reduced by two orders of magnitude (e.g., from 10 to 0.1). The range of parameters favourable to the evolution of IT narrows as $\beta$ decreases to 0.1, and then broadens with further decreases in $\beta$, particularly on the square lattice. } \label{fig_selection_strength}
elements.figure-2411-015: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.94\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_mutation_139} \includegraphics[width=0.23\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_unit_32} \end{center} \caption{ Robustness of evolutionary outcomes to the mutation rate, $\mu$. Note that outcomes remain qualitatively similar even when the mutation rate is increased tangibly from zero (e.g., up to $\mu=0.01$). } \label{fig_mutation_rate}
elements.figure-2411-016: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.94\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_population_size_140} \includegraphics[width=0.23\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_unit_32} \end{center} \caption{ Robustness of evolutionary outcomes to the population size, $Z$. elements.figure-2411-017: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.94\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_node_degree_136} \includegraphics[width=0.23\textwidth]{symmetric_NTG_Fig_unit_32} \end{center} \caption{ Robustness of evolutionary outcomes to variations in the mean node degree, $\langle d\rangle$.
